ground_truth.title: My LLM coding workflow going into 2026
ground_truth.author: Addy Osmani
ground_truth.main_content:
AI coding assistants became game-changers this year, but harnessing them effectively takes skill and structure. These tools dramatically increased what LLMs can do for real-world coding, and many developers embraced them. Using LLMs for programming is not a push-button magic experience - it's difficult and unintuitive and getting great results requires learning new patterns. Critical thinking remains key. I've converged on a workflow that treats the LLM as a powerful pair programmer that requires clear direction, context and oversight rather than autonomous judgment.

In this article, I share how I plan, code, and collaborate with AI going into 2026, distilling tips and best practices from my experience and the community's collective learning. It's a more disciplined AI-assisted engineering approach - leveraging AI aggressively while staying proudly accountable for the software produced.

Key principles include: starting with a clear plan and detailed specifications before coding; breaking work into small, iterative chunks rather than monolithic tasks; providing extensive context and guidance to the AI; choosing the right model for each task and being willing to switch models when needed; leveraging AI coding tools across the entire software development lifecycle; always keeping a human in the loop to verify, test, and review everything; committing code frequently and using version control as a safety net; customizing AI behavior with rules files and examples; embracing testing and automation as force multipliers; and continuously learning and adapting as AI amplifies existing skills.

The LLM is an assistant, not an autonomously reliable coder. I remain the senior dev; the LLM is there to accelerate me, not replace my judgment. In practical terms, I only merge or ship code after I've understood it. It's all about mindset: stay alert, test often, review always. It's still your codebase at the end of the day.
with: AI coding assistants became game-changers this year, but harnessing them effectively takes skill and structure.
I break the project into iterative steps or tickets and tackle them one by one, avoiding large, monolithic outputs.
LLMs are only as good as the context you provide - show them the relevant code, docs, and constraints.
If one model gets stuck or gives mediocre outputs, try another - use the best tool for the job.
The LLM is an assistant, not an autonomously reliable coder. I am the senior dev; the LLM is there to accelerate me, not replace my judgment.

items.caption: A full overview of where AI can improve the developer experience. This spans design, inner, submit, and outer loops - highlighting every point where AI can meaningfully reduce toil.
